title: Agentic AI addendum to the AI technical standard for Australian Government
description: Australian Government best-practice standard for secure and governed implementation of agentic AI systems.

source.title: Agentic AI addendum

content: The Agentic AI addendum provides best-practice guidance for Australian Government agencies exploring, developing or using agentic AI. It applies alongside the Australian Government AI technical standard and addresses agentic considerations across the AI lifecycle, including design, data, training, evaluation, integration, monitoring and decommissioning. The underlying technical-standard statements, criteria and general guidance continue to apply.
aiSummary: DTA addendum extending the Australian Government AI technical standard with secure and governed agentic-AI implementation guidance.

source.title: Agentic AI addendum

title: ASD Blueprint for Secure Cloud — Artificial intelligence usage policy requirement
description: ASD's Blueprint for Secure Cloud, first released on 19 December 2023 and last updated on 20 April 2026, surfaces an artificial intelligence usage policy as an organisational policy expected under ISM control ISM-2074.
content: ASD's Blueprint for Secure Cloud was initially launched on 19 December 2023 and last updated on 20 April 2026. It is better-practice guidance for designing, configuring and deploying secure cloud and hybrid workspaces, with a current focus on Microsoft 365. Its security and governance section links system security planning, the Essential Eight, system security plan annexes, and organisational policies and strategies. The organisational policies checklist explicitly identifies a general-purpose artificial intelligence usage policy as a requirement of ISM control ISM-2074. The Blueprint does not provide a template for this artefact, but positions it as part of the documentation agencies should review, develop and maintain when operating systems built using the Blueprint.
aiSummary: ASD Blueprint guidance, first launched in December 2023 and updated in April 2026, identifies a general-purpose AI usage policy as an ISM-2074 organisational policy requirement.

source.title: Organisational policies and strategies

verification.notes: The direct ASD checklist remains fingerprinted, but the primary changelog date evidence is not fingerprint-bound and requires editorial re-verification.

title: Policy for the Responsible Use of AI in Government
description: Commonwealth policy setting mandatory requirements for the safe, transparent, and accountable use of AI by non-corporate Commonwealth entities.
content: Version 2.0 took effect on 15 December 2025 and applies to all non-corporate Commonwealth entities, with specified exceptions. It requires accountable officials, transparency statements, a strategic approach to AI adoption, operational controls for responsible AI use, AI use-case accountability and registers, staff training, and AI use-case impact assessments.
aiSummary: Core Commonwealth policy governing responsible AI use by non-corporate Commonwealth entities from 15 December 2025.

source.title: Policy for the responsible use of AI in government - Version 2.0 | digital.gov.au

title: Artificial intelligence | 2025 Implementation Plan
description: Artificial intelligence chapter of the third annual Data and Digital Government Strategy Implementation Plan, covering APS AI adoption, governance, capability and delivery priorities.
content: The third annual Data and Digital Government Strategy Implementation Plan identifies artificial intelligence as one of four priorities for the coming year. Its AI chapter covers adoption across the Australian Public Service, responsible-use policy and governance, public- and private-sector use cases, productivity, and workforce capability. The official version 1.0 PDF says the plan was first published in November 2025, while the plan landing page was last updated on 17 November 2025. Policai therefore records the publication date at month precision rather than inventing a day.
aiSummary: AI chapter of the third annual Data and Digital Government Strategy Implementation Plan, first published in November 2025.

title: AI Plan for the Australian Public Service 2025
description: Australian Government plan for substantially increasing safe and responsible AI adoption across the APS through governance, workforce capability and secure tools.
content: Published in November 2025, the APS AI Plan is limited to AI adoption and use by Australian Government agencies rather than industry or personal use. It organises APS action around three pillars—Trust, People and Tools—and sets measures including foundational AI training, access to secure AI tools, Chief AI Officers, and tracking and reporting of AI use. The plan is jointly owned by the Department of Finance, Digital Transformation Agency and Australian Public Service Commission.
aiSummary: APS-wide AI adoption plan built around Trust, People and Tools, published in November 2025 and jointly owned by Finance, DTA and APSC.

title: National AI Plan
description: Whole-of-government strategic framework for growing Australia’s AI industry and guiding economy-wide AI adoption, capability, investment and safety.
agencies: Department of Industry, Science and Resources
content: Published on 2 December 2025, the National AI Plan sets Australian Government direction through three goals and nine actions: capture opportunities through smart infrastructure, domestic AI capability and investment; spread benefits through adoption, workforce skills and public services; and keep Australians safe through harm mitigation, responsible practices and global norms. It is a strategic framework rather than legislation. Implementation is iterative and evidence-based, supported by existing technology-neutral laws and sector regulators, and includes establishing the Australian AI Safety Institute.
aiSummary: Whole-of-government framework with three goals and nine actions for AI industry growth, broad adoption and safety.

source.title: National AI Plan | Department of Industry Science and Resources

title: Voluntary AI Safety Standard
description: Australia's first voluntary AI safety standard, published in September 2024 with 10 governance guardrails and updated in October 2025 through the Guidance for AI Adoption.
content: Published on 5 September 2024, Australia's first Voluntary AI Safety Standard set out 10 voluntary guardrails for organisations across the AI supply chain: accountability and governance; risk and impact management; data governance and security; testing and monitoring; human oversight; user transparency; contestability; supply-chain transparency; compliance records; and stakeholder engagement, fairness and inclusion. The standard did not create new legal obligations; it was designed to help organisations comply with existing Australian law, emerging guidance and community expectations. On 21 October 2025, the National AI Centre published the Guidance for AI Adoption, updating and simplifying the standard into 6 essential practices. This historical record is linked as superseded by that current guidance.
aiSummary: Historical 2024 voluntary standard of 10 AI governance guardrails, evolved into the Guidance for AI Adoption in October 2025.

source.title: Voluntary AI Safety Standard

title: Australia's AI Ethics Principles
description: Foundational Australian principles outlining responsible design, development, deployment, and oversight expectations for AI systems.
agencies: Department of Industry, Science and Resources
content: Australia's AI Ethics Principles were published on 7 November 2019 and updated on 2 December 2025. They set out 8 voluntary principles to guide businesses and governments in responsibly designing, developing, and implementing AI.
aiSummary: Foundational Australian AI ethics principles published in 2019 and maintained as a current reference point.

title: Government Response to the Privacy Act Review Report
description: Australian Government response to the Privacy Act Review, including implications for automated decision-making, profiling, and AI-enabled privacy harms.
content: The Australian Government released its response to the Privacy Act Review Report on 28 September 2023 and last updated the publication page on 12 March 2025. The response remains relevant to AI policy because it addresses profiling, automated decision-making, and other privacy reforms with downstream implications for AI systems.
aiSummary: Commonwealth privacy reform response published on 28 September 2023, with continuing relevance to AI and automated decision-making.

title: Introducing mandatory guardrails for AI in high-risk settings: proposals paper
description: DISR proposals paper (September 2024) on mandatory guardrails for AI in high-risk settings. Consultation closed 4 October 2024; the National AI Plan (2 December 2025) confirmed the government will not proceed with mandatory guardrails or a dedicated AI Act at this time.
agencies: Department of Industry, Science and Resources
content: The proposals paper "Introducing mandatory guardrails for AI in high-risk settings" was released on 5 September 2024 and consulted on options including a standalone AI Act. The consultation closed on 4 October 2024 and attracted more than 300 submissions. The National AI Plan, released 2 December 2025, confirmed the government will not proceed with mandatory guardrails at this time, relying instead on existing technology-neutral laws, sector regulators, voluntary guidance and the Australian AI Safety Institute.
aiSummary: Closed 2024 consultation on mandatory high-risk AI guardrails; the December 2025 National AI Plan confirmed the government is not proceeding.

verification.notes: An automated retrieval established this record's first source fingerprint after the recorded editorial review; fingerprinted editorial re-verification is required.

title: Positioning Australia as a leader in digital economy regulation — Automated Decision Making and AI Regulation
description: Historical 2022 issues paper on automated decision making and AI regulation. The consultation closed in May 2022; no dedicated ADM/AI regulation followed.
agencies: Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet
content: The Digital Technology Taskforce consultation on automated decision-making and AI regulation opened on 17 March 2022 and closed on 20 May 2022. It examined how Australia's regulatory settings could better support responsible use of AI and automated decision-making.
aiSummary: 2022 federal issues paper on regulatory settings for automated decision-making and AI.

title: NSW AI Assessment Framework
description: Mandatory assessment framework for NSW Government AI projects. A modernised framework, built by the NSW Office for AI with CSIRO’s Data61 and aligned to the national assurance framework and the EU AI Act, launched on 27 January 2026, replacing the version in force since 1 July 2024.
content: The NSW AI Assessment Framework (AIAF) is mandatory for NSW Government agencies’ AI projects. The original framework commenced 1 July 2024. On 27 January 2026 NSW launched a modernised AIAF developed by the NSW Office for AI (established September 2025) with CSIRO’s Data61, aligned with the National framework for the assurance of AI in government and international approaches including the EU AI Act. NSW also published an Australian-first guide to using AI agents in government on 20 October 2025.
aiSummary: Current mandatory NSW framework for assessing AI systems under Circular DCS-2024-04.

verification.notes: An automated retrieval established this record's first source fingerprint after the recorded editorial review; fingerprinted editorial re-verification is required.

content: This guideline was endorsed by the Victorian Secretaries Board on 24 September 2024 and made by the Secretary, Department of Premier and Cabinet, on 27 November 2024. The public page was updated on 12 February 2025. It sets minimum requirements for safe and responsible use of generative AI in the Victorian Public Sector.

title: Artificial intelligence governance policy
description: Queensland whole-of-government policy for AI governance under the Queensland Government Enterprise Architecture.
agencies: Department of Customer Services, Open Data and Small and Family Business
content: Queensland's current QGEA artificial intelligence governance policy is a current mandated policy, version 1.0.0, effective from September 2024. It requires agencies to use a consistent evidence-based process for evaluating AI and to establish AI governance arrangements based on ISO 38507. The page was last updated on 8 August 2025.
aiSummary: Current mandated Queensland AI governance policy under QGEA, effective from September 2024.

title: Use of generative AI in government
description: Queensland Government Enterprise Architecture fact sheet on safe and appropriate use of commercial generative AI tools in government work.
agencies: Department of Customer Services, Open Data and Small and Family Business
content: This current Queensland Government Enterprise Architecture fact sheet is version 1.0.1, dated July 2025. It applies existing Queensland Government obligations to commercial generative AI use and gives practical guidance on privacy and security, accuracy, legal risk, ethics and transparency, accountability, recordkeeping, risk assessment and human review of outputs.

content: Victoria's AI Mission Statement was released on 30 January 2026 and sets out the Victorian Government's plan to responsibly develop and adopt AI. It outlines six pillars covering investment and adoption, digital infrastructure, local innovation, talent and workforce, ethical AI use, and public sector adoption.

title: Artificial Intelligence Ethics Policy
description: South Australian across-government policy establishing the ethical framework for the design, development, deployment, and operation of AI-enabled systems and services.
content: The South Australian Artificial Intelligence Ethics Policy is an across-government mandatory policy approved on 10 October 2025. It applies to all South Australian public sector agencies and requires agencies to align AI-enabled systems and services with the Australian AI Ethics Principles and the National Framework for the Assurance of Artificial Intelligence in Government.

title: Guidance for the use of artificial intelligence in Tasmanian Government
description: Tasmanian Government guidance for responsible and risk-based use of artificial intelligence across government agencies.
content: Tasmania's AI guidance, revision 1.4 dated 23 July 2024, provides seven recommendations for agencies on responsible and risk-based use of AI in government. It aligns with the National Framework for the Assurance of Artificial Intelligence in Government and supports Tasmanian agencies with assurance, governance, procurement, capability, and glossary guidance.
aiSummary: Tasmanian Government AI guidance issued on 23 July 2024 for responsible and risk-based use of AI across government agencies.

title: Artificial Intelligence Assurance Framework
description: Northern Territory whole-of-government framework for responsible, risk-based use of AI in public services.
content: The Northern Territory Government Artificial Intelligence Assurance Framework, published in November 2025 and last updated on 10 December 2025, outlines ethics principles, a quick risk assessment, and an assurance process for agencies and government-owned corporations using AI-enabled systems. It applies stronger controls to medium and high-risk AI activities while enabling low-risk use with agency-level oversight.
aiSummary: Northern Territory whole-of-government AI assurance framework published in November 2025 for responsible and risk-based AI use in public services.

title: GPN-AI — Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence
description: Federal Court of Australia general practice note governing the use of generative AI by all persons appearing before or filing documents with the Court.
content: GPN-AI is a General Practice Note signed by Chief Justice Mortimer that sets out the Federal Court's expectations for use of generative AI in connection with proceedings. It applies to all persons who appear before or file documents with the Court, including litigants, witnesses, and third parties producing documents under subpoena. The practice note establishes three baseline expectations: users should understand AI capabilities, limitations and risks; use must not adversely affect the administration of justice; and disclosure of AI use may be required. It identifies heightened risk areas in pleadings and submissions (hallucinated citations, fictitious authorities) and evidentiary materials (affidavits, expert reports). Confidentiality obligations apply to information entered into AI tools. Non-compliance may attract adverse costs orders.
aiSummary: Federal Court practice note setting disclosure obligations and baseline expectations for generative AI use in proceedings, effective 16 April 2026.

title: SC Gen 23 — Use of Artificial Intelligence in Court Proceedings
description: Supreme Court of New South Wales practice note on the use of AI in proceedings before the Court.
agencies: Supreme Court of New South Wales
content: Practice Note SC Gen 23 governs the use of generative AI in NSW Supreme Court proceedings, including restrictions on using generative AI to draft affidavits, witness statements and expert reports. It was issued on 21 November 2024, reissued on 28 January 2025, and commenced on 3 February 2025. The Court published a review statement on 17 November 2025.
aiSummary: NSW Supreme Court practice note requiring disclosure of AI use in court proceedings and verification of AI-generated content.

source.title: Supreme Court Practice Note SC Gen 23

title: Guidelines on the Use of Artificial Intelligence in Court Proceedings
description: Guidelines for litigants on responsible AI use in Victorian Supreme Court proceedings, issued 6 May 2024. Replaced on 14 May 2026 by Practice Note SC GEN 25.
content: The Supreme Court of Victoria issued guidelines on the use of artificial intelligence in court proceedings. The guidelines require practitioners and self-represented litigants to disclose the use of AI tools in the preparation of documents filed with the Court. They emphasise that responsibility for the accuracy and completeness of all court documents remains with the person filing them, regardless of whether AI was used. The guidelines address specific risks including hallucinated case citations and fabricated legal propositions.
aiSummary: Victorian Supreme Court guidelines on AI use in proceedings, requiring disclosure and placing verification responsibility on practitioners.

verification.notes: An automated retrieval established this record's first source fingerprint after the recorded editorial review; fingerprinted editorial re-verification is required.

title: FCFCOA Practice Direction — Use of Artificial Intelligence (PD-AI)
description: Practice direction of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia on the use of AI, including generative AI, issued 29 May 2026 and applying to proceedings commenced on or after 1 December 2025 in Divisions 1 and 2.
agencies: Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia
content: FCFCOA Practice Direction: Use of Artificial Intelligence (PD-AI) was issued on 29 May 2026. It applies to litigants (including self-represented litigants), lawyers, experts and other court users in proceedings commenced on or after 1 December 2025. It requires AI use consistent with integrity, accuracy, accountability, professional obligations, confidentiality and data security, and prohibits inputting confidential or privileged material into public AI tools, with strict requirements around discovery, disclosure orders, suppression orders and subpoenas.
aiSummary: Federal Circuit and Family Court practice direction on AI use in proceedings, with emphasis on confidentiality in family law matters.

title: Practice Direction 5 of 2025 - Accuracy of References in Submissions
description: Queensland Supreme Court practice direction requiring named responsibility for submissions and verification of references, addressing risks from generative-AI hallucinations.
content: Practice Direction 5 of 2025 addresses the risk that generative AI may produce inaccurate or fictitious cases, legislation and other references, or inadequately checked language in submissions. It requires the responsible person for written and oral submissions to be identified, requires legal practitioners to verify the accuracy and relevance of references and exercise their own professional judgment, and tells self-represented litigants to verify references using authoritative legal resources. It warns that non-existent references may result in professional referral, personal costs consequences, adjournment or adverse costs.
aiSummary: Queensland Supreme Court direction requiring accountable authorship and verification of references in submissions because of generative-AI hallucination risk.

source.title: Supreme Court of Queensland Practice Direction 5 of 2025 - Accuracy of References in Submissions

verification.notes: Replaced a dead landing-page URL with the primary PDF and removed unsupported claims that the direction requires general AI-use disclosure for affidavits and expert reports.

source.title: Supreme Court of Queensland Practice Direction 5 of 2025 - Accuracy of References in Submissions

title: Frontier AI models and their impact on cyber security
description: Australian Signals Directorate guidance on the cyber security implications of increasingly capable frontier AI models, including Claude Mythos Preview.
content: ASD published guidance on 9 April 2026 about the cyber security implications of increasingly capable frontier AI models, following Anthropic's 7 April 2026 announcement of Claude Mythos Preview. The guidance says frontier models reduce the cost, effort and expertise required to discover and exploit software vulnerabilities, while also creating opportunities for defenders to identify and remediate vulnerabilities before exploitation. ASD advises organisations to maintain strong cyber security baselines aligned to the Information Security Manual and Essential Eight, reduce exposed attack paths, patch more frequently, use AI defensively where appropriate, and implement layered security. APRA's 30 April 2026 AI industry letter points regulated entities to ASD advice on frontier AI models.
aiSummary: ASD guidance on frontier AI cyber risks, defensive use of AI, patching tempo, attack-surface reduction, and layered cyber resilience.

source.title: Frontier models and their impact on cyber security

verification.notes: The official cyber.gov.au page confirms the title, publication date and the described offensive and defensive cyber implications.

source.title: Frontier models and their impact on cyber security

title: APRA Letter to Industry on Artificial Intelligence (AI)
description: APRA industry letter setting expectations for banks, insurers and superannuation trustees to strengthen governance, assurance, cyber security and operational resilience for AI-related risks.
content: APRA published a 30 April 2026 letter to industry after targeted supervisory engagement with selected large banks, insurers and superannuation trustees in late 2025. APRA found AI adoption accelerating across regulated industries while governance, risk management, assurance and operational resilience practices were not keeping pace. The letter says APRA is engaging across the sector on increased cyber threats from high capability frontier AI models such as Anthropic Mythos, and expects entities to manage AI risk through board literacy, risk appetite, oversight and accountability, stronger information security controls, timely patching, vulnerability remediation, attention to third-party and concentration risks, and credible fallback processes for critical operations. APRA says it may take stronger supervisory action or enforcement where entities fail to identify, manage or control AI risks proportionate to their size, scale and complexity.
aiSummary: APRA guidance requiring regulated financial entities to lift AI governance, assurance, cyber resilience and operational-risk controls in response to fast-moving AI risks including Anthropic Mythos.

verification.notes: An automated retrieval established this record's first source fingerprint after the recorded editorial review; fingerprinted editorial re-verification is required.

title: Guidance for AI Adoption
description: The National AI Centre’s Guidance for AI Adoption (21 October 2025) sets out six essential practices ("AI6") for safe and responsible AI, in Foundations and Implementation tiers. It supersedes the Voluntary AI Safety Standard.
agencies: Department of Industry, Science and Resources
content: Guidance for AI Adoption, published by the National AI Centre on 21 October 2025, replaces the Voluntary AI Safety Standard. It defines six essential practices (AI6) across two tiers — Foundations for organisations starting out, and Implementation practices for organisations governing AI at scale — covering accountability, risk management, data governance, testing, transparency and human oversight.
aiSummary: Current federal voluntary guidance: six essential practices for safe and responsible AI adoption, replacing the 2024 Voluntary AI Safety Standard.

title: Australian Government AI technical standard
description: DTA technical standard (31 July 2025) for designing, developing, deploying and decommissioning AI systems in government, structured around the Discover/Operate/Retire lifecycle.
content: The Australian Government AI technical standard, released by the DTA on 31 July 2025, gives agencies detailed technical requirements across the AI system lifecycle — from discovery and design through operation to retirement — supporting the Policy for the responsible use of AI in government.
aiSummary: DTA lifecycle technical standard for building and operating AI systems in the Australian Government.

title: National framework for the assurance of artificial intelligence in government
description: Joint Commonwealth, state and territory assurance framework agreed by Data and Digital Ministers on 21 June 2024, with five cornerstones mapped to Australia’s AI Ethics Principles.
content: The National framework for the assurance of artificial intelligence in government was agreed by Data and Digital Ministers on 21 June 2024. It establishes a nationally consistent approach to AI assurance across the Commonwealth, states and territories, built on five cornerstones with practices mapped to the eight AI Ethics Principles. Jurisdictional frameworks (such as the NSW AIAF and WA framework) align to it.
aiSummary: Cross-jurisdictional assurance framework aligning all Australian governments’ AI oversight to the AI Ethics Principles.

title: OAIC guidance on privacy and developing and training generative AI models
description: OAIC guidance (21 October 2024) on how the Privacy Act and APPs apply to developing and training generative AI models.
agencies: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner
content: Published 21 October 2024 alongside companion guidance on commercial AI products, this OAIC guidance explains how the Privacy Act 1988 and the Australian Privacy Principles apply when organisations develop or fine-tune generative AI models, including collection, use and disclosure of personal information in training data.
aiSummary: OAIC guidance applying the Privacy Act to the development and training of generative AI models.

verification.notes: An automated retrieval established this record's first source fingerprint after the recorded editorial review; fingerprinted editorial re-verification is required.

title: OAIC guidance on privacy and the use of commercially available AI products
description: OAIC guidance (21 October 2024) for organisations deploying commercial AI products consistently with the Privacy Act and APPs.
agencies: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner
content: Published 21 October 2024, this OAIC guidance addresses privacy obligations when organisations select and use commercially available AI products, including due diligence, transparency to individuals, and handling of personal information entered into AI tools.
aiSummary: OAIC guidance on privacy obligations when deploying commercial AI products.

verification.notes: An automated retrieval established this record's first source fingerprint after the recorded editorial review; fingerprinted editorial re-verification is required.

title: Online safety industry codes covering generative AI and AI companions
description: Enforceable eSafety industry codes registered in September 2025 — including for generative AI services and AI companion chatbots — in effect from 9 March 2026, with age-assurance obligations and penalties up to $49.5m.
content: In September 2025 the eSafety Commissioner registered six new industry codes under the Online Safety Act, including codes covering generative AI services and AI companion chatbots. The codes took effect on 9 March 2026 and impose age-assurance and safety obligations, with civil penalties of up to $49.5 million. In March 2026 eSafety reported on risks AI companions pose to children following transparency notices issued to four providers in October 2025.
aiSummary: Enforceable online-safety codes for generative AI and AI companion services, in force since March 2026.

title: WA Government Artificial Intelligence Policy and Assurance Framework
description: Western Australia’s whole-of-government AI policy and assurance framework (first published 5 March 2024; policy version 2 released July 2025), with AI Accountable Officers, mandatory risk self-assessments and an AI Advisory Board review of higher-risk projects.
agencies: Department of the Premier and Cabinet
content: The WA Government Artificial Intelligence Policy and Assurance Framework was first published on 5 March 2024 by the Office of Digital Government. It requires agencies to appoint AI Accountable Officers, complete risk self-assessments for AI use cases, and refer higher-risk projects to the WA AI Advisory Board. Version 2 of the policy was released in July 2025.
aiSummary: WA’s mandatory AI policy and assurance framework with accountable officers and risk-based review.

verification.notes: An automated retrieval established this record's first source fingerprint after the recorded editorial review; fingerprinted editorial re-verification is required.

title: ACT Government Artificial Intelligence Policy
description: The ACT Government’s AI policy (version 0.1, July 2024) with a framework and self-assessment tool for responsible AI use in the territory public service.
content: The ACT Government Artificial Intelligence Policy (version 0.1, published July 2024) sets out principles and a self-assessment approach for responsible AI use across the ACT public service. The effective date shown reflects the July 2024 publication month; the ACT has not published a day-level commencement date.
aiSummary: ACT public service AI policy with principles and a self-assessment tool (v0.1, July 2024).

title: Practice Note SC GEN 25 — Use of Artificial Intelligence by Court Users
description: Victorian Supreme Court practice note (14 May 2026) governing AI use by court users, replacing the May 2024 guidelines; accompanied by new judicial guidelines.
content: Practice Note SC GEN 25, issued by the Supreme Court of Victoria on 14 May 2026, governs the use of artificial intelligence by court users in proceedings. It replaces the Court’s May 2024 guidelines for litigants and was issued alongside updated guidance for judicial officers.
aiSummary: Victoria’s current Supreme Court practice note on AI use in proceedings, replacing the 2024 guidelines.

verification.notes: An automated retrieval established this record's first source fingerprint after the recorded editorial review; fingerprinted editorial re-verification is required.

title: Guidelines for the use of Generative AI in South Australian courts
description: Guidelines approved by the Supreme Court judges for generative AI use in litigation, applying to civil and criminal proceedings from 1 January 2026 across South Australian courts.
agencies: Courts Administration Authority of South Australia
content: Following a May 2025 consultation launched by the Chief Justice, the judges of the Supreme Court of South Australia approved guidelines for the use of generative AI in litigation, applying to any step taken in civil or criminal proceedings from 1 January 2026 in the Supreme, District, Magistrates, Youth and Environment, Resources and Development Courts. The guidelines give examples of ethical and responsible use and improper use, and were announced on 19 January 2026.
aiSummary: SA courts’ generative AI guidelines, effective 1 January 2026 across the state’s courts.
